(Parkwood/Columbia/Sony via AP) (ASSOCIATED PRESS) NEW YORK (AP) \u2014 Ten of the top albums of the year, as chosen by Associated Press Music Writer Maria Sherman. \"Cowboy Carter,\" Beyonc\u00e9 She rode in on a white horse, in patriotic chaps and wielding an American flag. She declared, this \"ain't a Country album\" but \"a 'Beyonc\u00e9' album\" \u2014 positioning herself in opposition to the genre's rigid power structures. And yet, she made the year's best country (and then some) release: Beyonc\u00e9's \"Act ll: Cowboy Carter,\" a 78-minute, 27-track masterclass in inherited and uncelebrated histories, pulling from the Black and brown performers at the core of country's canon, and providing visibility to oft-overlooked progenitors. It's heard in the inclusion of Linda Martell, the first Black woman to play the Grand Ole Opry. And it's heard in Beyonc\u00e9's natural twang, a return to form for the Texas native \u2014 at times, a vibrato that pulls directly from the earth. It may only be 2024, but it's hard to imagine this record not being considered one of the decade's best. \"BRAT,\" Charli XCX It wasn't just brat summer, babes, but a brat movement: English singer-songwriter Charli XCX's sixth album altered the language of 2024. The music oscillates between hedonism and anxiety \u2014 \"the euphoria of a late night on the dancefloor and the creeping disquietude of the morning after,\" as the AP reviewed it \u2014 but also manages to bring the underground's pop queen into the mainstream without compromising her vision. There was a period where Charli XCX's collaborations with the future-seeking PC Music collective and producer A .G. Cook seemed too progressive for everyday ears. But \"Brat,\" and its vomit-chartreuse iconography, reson"}]

Answer:

response:
Based on the search results, the 2024 Beyoncé album that broke streaming records with its blend of country-pop and Americana is **"Cowboy Carter"** (full title: "Act II: Cowboy Carter").

Released on March 29, 2024, this 78-minute, 27-track album showcased Beyoncé's exploration of country music, Americana, and the often-overlooked Black and brown performers at the core of country's history. The album featured contributions from Linda Martell, the first Black woman to play at the Grand Ole Opry, and highlighted Beyoncé's natural Texas twang. While Beyoncé herself declared it "ain't a Country album" but "a 'Beyoncé' album," it was widely praised as one of the year's best country releases and is considered a strong contender for one of the decade's best albums.
